questions.specification: A gaming company is developing a new mobile game.

options.content: ARM processors are generally more suitable for mobile gaming devices than x86 processors because ARM typically offers better power efficiency and generates less heat.
options.markscheme: Correct: ARM designs are typically more power-efficient, improving battery life and reducing thermal output, which suits mobile devices.

options.content: x86 processors are generally more suitable for mobile gaming devices than ARM processors because x86 uses less power and therefore gives better battery life.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: x86 is not typically associated with lower power consumption in mobile contexts compared to ARM.

options.content: Using RGB with 8 bits per channel means the display can show $2^8=256$ different colours in total.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: $2^8=256$ is per channel; total colours are $2^8\times 2^8\times 2^8=2^{24}=16\,777\,216$.

options.content: A mobile operating system manages memory for a game mainly by allowing the game to directly access and overwrite any memory location to maximise performance.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: Operating systems use memory protection and process isolation to prevent one app from overwriting other apps/OS memory.

questions.specification: A multi-user database server implements various memory management strategies.

parts.content:
Complete the memory management strategy comparison table.

| Strategy           | Page Size | Allocation Method | Fragmentation Type | Overhead | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fixed Partitioning  | N/A       |                   | Internal           | Low      |               |
| Dynamic Partitioning| N/A       | First Fit         |                    | Medium   |               |
| Paging             | 4 KB      |                   | Internal           | High     |               |
| Segmentation       | Variable  |                   | External           | High     |               |
parts.markscheme:
- Fixed partitioning: allocation method = static blocks / fixed-size partitions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fixed partitioning: best use case = simple / embedded systems <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dynamic partitioning: fragmentation type = external <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dynamic partitioning: best use case = variable-sized processes / programs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Paging: allocation method = page table(s) / page tables <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Paging: best use case = virtual memory systems <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Segmentation: allocation method = segment table(s) / segment tables <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Segmentation: best use case = logical program structure / separation into code, data, stack <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate virtual memory advantages and disadvantages for database servers.
parts.markscheme:
- Advantage: allows programs/databases to use more memory than is physically available (via swapping/secondary storage) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Advantage: process isolation/protection between processes (separate address spaces) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Advantage: can support efficient file I/O via memory mapping (e.g., memory-mapped files) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage: page faults/swapping can cause slowdowns and unpredictable response times (thrashing) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage: OS caching may compete with the database’s own buffer/cache management <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage: managing virtual memory adds overhead (e.g., extra address translation work) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A hospital implements a patient monitoring system with multiple real-time requirements. The system must handle various physiological sensors and logging tasks using real-time scheduling concepts.

parts.content:
A hospital implements a patient monitoring system. Complete the following table for five concurrent processes using **fixed-priority scheduling based on shortest period = highest priority**.

Then choose the appropriate **System Response** for each process from the following list, using the **severity order given here (most urgent to least urgent)**:
1. Emergency response
2. Critical alarm
3. Warning alert
4. Status update
5. Record to database

| Process | Execution Time | Period | Deadline | Priority Level | System Response |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heart Monitor | $2\text{ ms}$ | $10\text{ ms}$ | $10\text{ ms}$ | | |
| Blood Pressure | $12\text{ ms}$ | $60\text{ ms}$ | $60\text{ ms}$ | | |
| Temperature | $6\text{ ms}$ | $30\text{ ms}$ | $30\text{ ms}$ | | |
| Alarm System | $1\text{ ms}$ | $5\text{ ms}$ | $5\text{ ms}$ | | |
| Data Logging | $30\text{ ms}$ | $300\text{ ms}$ | $300\text{ ms}$ | | |
parts.markscheme:
- Alarm System has priority level 1 (highest) because it has the shortest period ($5\text{ ms}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Heart Monitor has priority level 2 because it has the next shortest period ($10\text{ ms}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Temperature has priority level 3 because it has the next shortest period ($30\text{ ms}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Blood Pressure has priority level 4 because it has the next shortest period ($60\text{ ms}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Data Logging has priority level 5 (lowest) because it has the longest period ($300\text{ ms}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Emergency response assigned to Alarm System (highest priority / most urgent per given severity order) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Critical alarm assigned to Heart Monitor <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Warning alert assigned to Temperature <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Status update assigned to Blood Pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Record to database assigned to Data Logging <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why a hospital patient monitoring system might use fixed-priority scheduling (shortest period = highest priority) rather than a simple first-come-first-served approach. In your answer, refer to predictability and the need to prioritise urgent tasks.
parts.markscheme:
- States that fixed-priority scheduling is more predictable because priorities are defined in advance (static) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that more urgent/critical tasks (e.g. alarms) can be guaranteed to run before less urgent tasks (e.g. logging) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Contrasts with first-come-first-served where a long/low-importance task could delay a critical one (poor responsiveness) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Applies to the medical context: delays could affect patient safety, so prioritisation is necessary <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A data center operates thousands of servers continuously.

parts.content: Explain why redundant power supplies and cooling systems are essential in data centers.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that redundant systems ensure continuous operation: data centers must run 24/7 without interruption <marks>1</marks>
- Explain that they provide hardware protection: preventing damage from power failures and overheating <marks>1</marks>
- State that redundant systems maintain service availability: maintaining uptime for critical services and applications <marks>1</marks>
- Explain that they assist in cost prevention: avoiding expensive downtime and data loss <marks>1</marks>
- Describe how they provide fault tolerance: the system continues operating even if components fail <marks>1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define continuous operation requirements
steps.content: In the context of operational reliability, data centers are designed for **24/7 continuous operation**. Because they host critical applications and data for thousands of users, any amount of downtime is unacceptable. Redundant systems ensure that there is no "single point of failure" that could take the entire facility offline.

highlights.text: operates thousands of servers continuously

steps.title: Explain fault tolerance
steps.content:
Redundant power supplies and cooling systems provide **fault tolerance**. This is the ability of a system to continue operating properly even if one or more of its components fail. 

*   **Power:** If one power grid or supply unit fails, a secondary source (like a backup generator or UPS) takes over immediately.
*   **Cooling:** If one air conditioning unit breaks, others increase their output to maintain the necessary temperature.

steps.title: Ensure service availability
steps.content: From an availability standpoint, these systems protect the **uptime** of services. Without redundant cooling, servers would rapidly overheat, leading to automatic thermal shutdowns or permanent hardware damage. By maintaining a stable environment and constant power, the data center ensures that digital services remain accessible to users at all times, avoiding the massive financial and data losses associated with unexpected outages.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 3 marks, your explanation should focus on:
1.  **Continuous Operation:** The need for 24/7 functionality without interruption.
2.  **Fault Tolerance:** The system's ability to remain operational despite component failure.
3.  **Service Availability/Protection:** Preventing hardware damage and maintaining uptime for critical applications.

steps.title: Understanding Redundancy as Protection
steps.content:
In the context of a data center, **redundancy** means having secondary systems (like extra power units or cooling fans) that are ready to take over immediately if the primary system fails. 

From a **risk management** perspective, this is not just about convenience; it's about protecting the massive investment in hardware and preventing the catastrophic financial consequences of a system crash.

steps.title: Mitigating Physical Hardware Damage
steps.content:
The first major risk is physical destruction. Servers contain delicate electronic components that generate significant heat. 

*   **Cooling Systems:** If cooling fails, temperatures rise rapidly, leading to **overheating**. This can cause permanent hardware damage (melting circuits) or trigger emergency thermal shutdowns. 
*   **Power Supplies:** Redundant power protects against power surges or sudden drops that can fry motherboard components.

By having backups, we ensure these environmental conditions remain stable, protecting the physical equipment from failure.

highlights.text: preventing damage from power failures and overheating

steps.title: Ensuring Fault Tolerance
steps.content:
Redundancy provides **fault tolerance**, which is the ability of a system to continue operating even when one of its components fails. 

In a data center, services must be available $24/7$. If a power supply unit (PSU) fails in a single server, a redundant PSU takes over the load instantly. This ensures **continuous operation** and maintains service availability for the users who rely on that data.

highlights.text: maintaining uptime for critical services and applications
the system continues operating even if components fail

steps.title: Mitigating Economic Impact
steps.content:
The final, and often most critical, reason is the **economic impact**. Downtime is incredibly expensive for businesses. 

$$\text{Total Cost of Failure} = \text{Lost Revenue} + \text{Data Recovery Costs} + \text{SLA Penalties}$$

If a data center goes offline, a company might lose thousands of dollars per minute in sales. Furthermore, sudden power loss can lead to **data corruption**, which is costly to repair and can lead to legal liabilities or loss of customer trust. Redundancy acts as financial insurance against these high costs.

highlights.text: avoiding expensive downtime and data loss

steps.title: Define continuous operation requirements
steps.content:
In the context of **Topic A2.2.2 (Function of Servers)**, data centers are the backbone of modern digital infrastructure. They are expected to operate 24/7 without any interruption. 

Redundancy means having a 'backup' component ready to take over immediately if the primary one fails. This creates **fault tolerance**, allowing the system to continue running despite individual component failures.

highlights.text: data centers must run 24/7 without interruption

steps.title: Analyze power redundancy functions
steps.content:
Using a functional analysis, we look at what happens if power is lost. Without redundant power (like Uninterruptible Power Supplies or backup generators):
1. **Data Integrity:** Sudden shutdowns can cause 'bit rot' or database corruption. Redundant power ensures transactions are completed safely.
2. **Continuous Uptime:** Critical services (like healthcare or banking apps) must remain available. Redundant power prevents the 'blackout' that would take these services offline.

steps.title: Analyze cooling redundancy functions
steps.content:
Servers generate immense amounts of heat. If a cooling unit fails without a redundant backup:
1. **Thermal Safety:** Components would quickly exceed safe operating temperatures, leading to emergency 'thermal throttling' or total system shutdown.
2. **Hardware Longevity:** Consistent cooling prevents physical damage to delicate circuitry (CPU/GPU). Redundant cooling ensures that even during maintenance or a fan failure, the hardware remains protected from heat-related wear.

steps.title: Evaluate the economic impact
steps.content:
Finally, these systems assist in **cost prevention**. The financial loss from just one hour of downtime or the cost of replacing thousands of heat-damaged servers far outweighs the investment in redundant infrastructure. 

By providing **fault tolerance**, the data center maintains its Service Level Agreements (SLAs) and avoids expensive data recovery processes.

highlights.text: avoiding expensive downtime and data loss

parts.content: The servers process data using parallel computing. Outline how parallel processing differs from sequential processing.
parts.markscheme:
- Outline the difference in execution: parallel involves multiple operations performed simultaneously, whereas sequential involves one operation at a time <marks>1</marks>
- Outline the difference in speed and task type: parallel is faster for tasks that can be divided, whereas sequential has a simpler programming model <marks>1</marks>
- Outline the difference in coordination: parallel requires coordination between processors, whereas sequential needs no coordination <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the Execution Flow
steps.content:
The fundamental difference lies in **how and when** instructions are executed. 

*   **Sequential Processing:** Instructions are executed one after another in a linear, serial fashion. The CPU must complete one operation before moving to the next.
*   **Parallel Processing:** Multiple instructions are executed **simultaneously** by utilizing multiple processors or cores. Large tasks are broken down into smaller sub-tasks that run at the exact same time.

highlights.text: parallel involves multiple operations performed simultaneously, whereas sequential involves one operation at a time

steps.title: Compare Speed and Suitability
steps.content:
Because instructions are processed at the same time, parallel processing is significantly faster for complex tasks that can be divided (like data analysis in a data center). 

However, **sequential processing** is often used for simpler tasks or when instructions depend on the result of the previous one, making it easier to program because you don't have to worry about the order of completion.

highlights.text: parallel is faster for tasks that can be divided, whereas sequential has a simpler programming model

steps.title: Contrast Coordination Requirements
steps.content:
In a parallel system, there is a "management overhead." The system must **coordinate** between different processors to ensure they don't interfere with each other and that the final results are combined correctly. 

In contrast, **sequential processing** requires no coordination because there is only one stream of instructions being handled by a single processing unit.

highlights.text: parallel requires coordination between processors, whereas sequential needs no coordination

steps.title: Define Execution Methods
steps.content:
The fundamental difference lies in how tasks are executed over time. 

*   **Sequential processing** follows a single-path logic where one instruction is executed at a time, in a linear order.
*   **Parallel processing** involves multiple operations being performed simultaneously across different processors or cores.

In the context of a data center, this means many servers (or many cores within a server) work on different parts of a problem at the exact same time.

highlights.text: parallel involves multiple operations performed simultaneously, whereas sequential involves one operation at a time

steps.title: Compare Resource Management
steps.content:
From a management perspective, the complexity of the programming and hardware coordination differs significantly:

*   **Coordination:** Parallel processing requires complex **resource management** to divide a large task into sub-tasks and coordinate the results between processors. Sequential processing requires no such coordination because there is only one execution path.
*   **Programming Logic:** Sequential systems use a simpler programming model because the developer doesn't need to worry about processors "waiting" for each other or data conflicts. Parallel systems, while faster for large divisible tasks, require much more sophisticated architectural planning to ensure all resources are used efficiently.

highlights.text: parallel requires coordination between processors, whereas sequential needs no coordination
sequential has a simpler programming model

steps.title: Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks, you should clearly contrast the two methods. 

1.  **Execution:** Parallel is simultaneous; sequential is one-by-one.
2.  **Management/Coordination:** Parallel requires dividing and coordinating tasks among multiple processors, while sequential is a simpler, single-stream process.

This concept relates to **Topic A1.1.6 (Pipelining in Multi-Core Architectures)** and **A1.3.5 (Resource Allocation)** in your IB syllabus.

parts.content: Describe how virtualization technology allows multiple operating systems to run on a single physical server.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe the role of the hypervisor: a software layer that manages virtual machines <marks>1</marks>
- Describe resource allocation: dividing physical resources (CPU, memory, storage) among virtual machines <marks>1</marks>
- Describe isolation: each virtual machine operates independently without affecting others <marks>1</marks>
- Describe hardware abstraction: virtual machines see virtualized hardware rather than physical hardware <marks>1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define the hypervisor's role
steps.content: In a hypervisor-centric view, the **hypervisor** (or Virtual Machine Monitor) is the essential software layer that sits between the physical hardware and the operating systems. Its primary job is to create and manage multiple Virtual Machines (VMs) on a single physical host.

highlights.text: multiple operating systems to run on a single physical server

steps.title: Allocate physical resources
steps.content: The hypervisor is responsible for **resource allocation**. It dynamically divides the server's physical resources—such as the CPU, RAM, and storage—and distributes them among the different guest operating systems. Each OS "thinks" it has its own dedicated hardware, but the hypervisor is actually brokering these resources in the background.

steps.title: Provide abstraction and isolation
steps.content: The hypervisor provides **hardware abstraction**, meaning the operating systems interact with virtual versions of hardware rather than the physical components directly. Furthermore, it ensures **isolation**, keeping each VM in a secure sandbox. This prevents one operating system from accessing the data of another or causing the entire server to crash if it fails.

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 3 marks, your description should include:
1. The **hypervisor** as the management software layer.
2. The process of **dividing/allocating resources** (CPU, memory) to different VMs.
3. The concept of **isolation** or **abstraction**, where each VM operates independently and sees virtualized hardware.

steps.title: Introduce the Hypervisor
steps.content:
The foundation of virtualization is a software layer called the **hypervisor** (or Virtual Machine Monitor). Its role is to act as an intermediary between the physical server hardware and the multiple operating systems. 

By using **hardware abstraction**, the hypervisor hides the physical characteristics of the server and instead presents a set of "virtual hardware" (like a virtual CPU and virtual RAM) to each operating system. This is a key concept in **Topic A1.3: Operating systems**, where we look at how software manages hardware resources.

highlights.text: the hypervisor: a software layer that manages virtual machines
virtual machines see virtualized hardware rather than physical hardware

steps.title: Partitioning Physical Resources
steps.content:
Once the hardware is abstracted, the hypervisor performs **partitioning**. This involves dividing the total pool of physical resources—specifically the CPU, memory (RAM), and storage—into smaller, isolated segments. 

Each segment is allocated to a specific Virtual Machine (VM). This ensures that the physical server's capacity is shared efficiently, allowing multiple operating systems to run simultaneously without one hogging all the resources.

highlights.text: dividing physical resources (CPU, memory, storage) among virtual machines

steps.title: Ensuring Isolation
steps.content:
The final step in this process is **isolation**. Because the hypervisor manages the partitioning, each virtual machine operates in its own independent environment. 

This means:
- One guest OS cannot see or access the data of another guest OS.
- If one operating system crashes, it does not affect the performance or stability of the others.
- To each OS, it feels as though it is running on its own dedicated, independent machine.

highlights.text: each virtual machine operates independently without affecting others

questions.specification: A smart city traffic management system coordinates multiple intersection controllers.

parts.content:
Complete the following table regarding the distributed system architecture for traffic coordination:

| System Component | Processing Power | Communication Protocol | Response Time | Fault Tolerance | Primary Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Central Controller | (i) | TCP/IP, MQTT | $< 1\text{ s}$ | High | (ii) |
| Intersection Node | Low | (iii) | $< 100\text{ ms}$ | Medium | Local traffic control |
| (iv) | Medium | Priority channels | $< 10\text{ ms}$ | Critical | Emergency response |
| (v) | High | HTTP/REST APIs | $> 1\text{ s}$ | Low | (vi) |
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1</marks> for each correct design element up to <marks>6</marks>:

| Label | Design Element |
| :--- | :--- |
| (i) | High |
| (ii) | System coordination |
| (iii) | Wireless/Ethernet |
| (iv) | Emergency Override |
| (v) | Data Analytics |
| (vi) | Pattern analysis |

**Full Design Table for Reference:**

| System Component | Processing Power | Communication Protocol | Response Time | Fault Tolerance | Primary Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Central Controller | High | TCP/IP, MQTT | $< 1\text{ s}$ | High | System coordination |
| Intersection Node | Low | Wireless/Ethernet | $< 100\text{ ms}$ | Medium | Local traffic control |
| Emergency Override | Medium | Priority channels | $< 10\text{ ms}$ | Critical | Emergency response |
| Data Analytics | High | HTTP/REST APIs | $> 1\text{ s}$ | Low | Pattern analysis |

steps.title: Define the Hierarchy Layers
steps.content:
To solve this, we use **Distributed Hierarchy Mapping**. This method categorizes system components based on their proximity to the 'real world' versus high-level business logic. 

In a distributed control system, we generally see four layers:
1.  **Field/Safety Layer**: Ultra-fast (ms), critical safety (Emergency Override).
2.  **Control Layer**: Local real-time decisions (Intersection Nodes).
3.  **Supervisory Layer**: Coordination of multiple local nodes (Central Controller).
4.  **Enterprise Layer**: Long-term data processing and high-level logic (Data Analytics).

This question is covered in **Topic A1.3: Operating systems and control systems** (specifically control system components and real-world applications).

steps.title: Analyze the Supervisory Layer
steps.content:
The **Central Controller** sits at the Supervisory Layer. It must handle incoming data from hundreds of intersections via protocols like MQTT. 

*   **(i) Processing Power**: To manage a whole city's traffic grid and coordinate many nodes, it requires **High** processing power.
*   **(ii) Primary Function**: Since it sits above the local nodes, its main job is **System coordination**.

highlights.text: Central Controller

steps.title: Determine Local Node Protocols
steps.content:
The **Intersection Node** is the Control Layer. It needs to communicate with sensors and the central system with a low response time ($< 100\text{ ms}$).

*   **(iii) Communication Protocol**: For local infrastructure like traffic lights, we use reliable, standard networking like **Wireless** (e.g., 5G/Zigbee) or **Ethernet**.

highlights.text: Intersection Node

steps.title: Identify the Field/Safety Layer
steps.content:
Row 3 shows a component with a **Critical** fault tolerance and a very fast response time of $< 10\text{ ms}$. This represents the Field/Safety layer where failures are not an option.

*   **(iv) System Component**: In traffic management, the component that must act instantly regardless of network congestion is the **Emergency Override** (used by sirens or manual police control).

highlights.text: Critical
< 10 ms

steps.title: Map the Enterprise Layer
steps.content:
The final row has a long response time ($> 1\text{ s}$) but uses web-based APIs (HTTP/REST). This describes the Enterprise Layer, which focuses on big-picture data rather than immediate light changes.

*   **(v) System Component**: This is **Data Analytics**. It uses high processing power to crunch historical data.
*   **(vi) Primary Function**: The goal of this layer is **Pattern analysis** (e.g., identifying when congestion usually peaks to adjust future timing plans).

highlights.text: HTTP/REST APIs

steps.title: Analyze the Central Controller
steps.content:
Using the **Functional Role Deduction** approach, we look at what the **Central Controller** does for the whole city. Since it must manage and coordinate data from multiple intersections, its **Primary Function (ii)** is **System coordination**.

Because it handles a massive amount of data and global logic for the entire smart city, we can infer that its **Processing Power (i)** must be **High** to maintain a response time of $< 1\text{ s}$.

highlights.text: Central Controller

steps.title: Determine Intersection Node Protocols
steps.content:
The **Intersection Node** performs 'Local traffic control' with 'Low' processing power. To send its local status to the central system and receive instructions, it requires standard networking infrastructure. 

In a distributed traffic system, the **Communication Protocol (iii)** used for the physical and link layers is typically **Wireless** (like 5G/Wi-Fi) or **Ethernet**.

highlights.text: Local traffic control

steps.title: Identify the Emergency Component
steps.content:
We look at the row where the function is **Emergency response**. This role requires 'Critical' fault tolerance and an extremely fast response time ($< 10\text{ ms}$) to ensure safety. 

From this functional role, we can deduce that the **System Component (iv)** is the **Emergency Override** system.

highlights.text: Emergency response

steps.title: Identify Data Analytics Role
steps.content:
The final row shows a component that uses 'HTTP/REST APIs', has a slow response time ($> 1\text{ s}$), but 'High' processing power. 

This functional profile (high power but not time-critical) describes a background processing layer. Therefore, the **System Component (v)** is **Data Analytics**, and its **Primary Function (vi)** is **Pattern analysis** (using the gathered data to improve future traffic flow).

highlights.text: HTTP/REST APIs

steps.title: Final Table Summary
steps.content:
By logically matching the system requirements (processing, speed, and reliability) to their specific roles, we complete the table:

*   **(i)** High
*   **(ii)** System coordination
*   **(iii)** Wireless/Ethernet
*   **(iv)** Emergency Override
*   **(v)** Data Analytics
*   **(vi)** Pattern analysis

parts.content: Explain how the system maintains coordination when communication links between intersections fail.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1</marks> for each correct explanation up to <marks>2</marks>:

- **Local autonomy**: Intersection nodes operate independently using pre-programmed timing patterns when communication with the central controller is lost. <marks>1</marks>
- **Backup protocols**: Implementation of default safe operating modes (e.g., all-way flashing yellow/red) ensures safety when coordination is impossible. <marks>1</marks>
- **Mesh networking**: Use of alternative communication paths through neighboring intersections to bypass a broken link and maintain a connection to the network. <marks>1</marks>
- **Graceful degradation**: The system maintains basic traffic flow functionality even if high-level optimization or data analytics are unavailable. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define Local Autonomy
steps.content:
In a smart city, intersections are usually managed by a central controller. However, if the communication link fails, the system switches to **local autonomy**. This means each intersection controller (an embedded computer) starts making decisions on its own rather than waiting for instructions from the central hub.

This is a form of **graceful degradation**, where the system continues to function at a basic level even when advanced features (like city-wide synchronization) are offline.

highlights.text: Local autonomy
Graceful degradation

steps.title: Use Pre-programmed Patterns
steps.content:
To maintain traffic flow without a central connection, each local controller uses **pre-programmed timing patterns** stored in its non-volatile memory. 

These patterns are based on historical data (e.g., a specific cycle for morning rush hour and another for late-night traffic). Because these programs are stored locally, the intersection can continue to cycle through green and red lights independently based on the time of day.

highlights.text: pre-programmed timing patterns

steps.title: Implement Backup Safety Protocols
steps.content:
If the local controller determines that its internal logic cannot safely manage the current traffic (or if sensors also fail), it triggers **backup protocols**. 

A common safety mode is switching the intersection to a flashing state (e.g., all-way flashing red or yellow). This ensures that the system fails safely, alerting drivers to treat the intersection as a manual stop rather than providing potentially conflicting signals.

highlights.text: Backup protocols

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To earn the **2 marks** for this question, you should describe two distinct local mechanisms:

1.  **Local Autonomy**: Explain that nodes operate independently using stored patterns when the connection is lost.
2.  **Backup Protocols/Safety Modes**: Explain the switch to default safe operations (like flashing lights) to ensure safety if coordination is completely lost.

steps.title: Identify the coordination problem
steps.content: In a smart city, intersection controllers must exchange data (like traffic flow rates) to synchronize lights. If a direct communication link between two intersections fails due to hardware issues or interference, this coordination is normally lost. To prevent this, the system can use **Network Redundancy**.

steps.title: Implement Mesh Networking
steps.content:
By using a **mesh network topology**, each intersection controller is connected to multiple other neighboring nodes rather than just one. This creates redundant physical or wireless paths for data to travel. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic A2.2.1 (Network Topologies)**, where a mesh ensures that no single point of failure can disconnect a node from the rest of the network.

highlights.text: Mesh networking

steps.title: Reroute data through neighbors
steps.content:
When a specific link fails, the system uses **dynamic routing protocols** to identify the failure and automatically reroute data packets. 

For example, if the direct path from $Intersection A$ to $Intersection B$ is broken, the system can route the data through $Intersection C$ ($A \rightarrow C \rightarrow B$). This bypasses the broken link and ensures the controllers stay synchronized.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks for this approach, your explanation should focus on:
1.  The use of **mesh networking** to provide alternative physical paths.
2.  The ability to **reroute data** through neighboring nodes to bypass the failure and maintain the connection.

highlights.text: Mesh networking: Use of alternative communication paths through neighboring intersections to bypass a broken link and maintain a connection to the network.

steps.title: Identify the coordination failure
steps.content: In a smart city, intersections usually talk to each other to optimize traffic flow. However, if the communication links fail, the system loses its ability to synchronize lights. To prevent dangerous situations, the system must transition from an "optimized state" to a **Safety-First Failover Mode**.

highlights.text: communication links between intersections fail

steps.title: Transition to backup protocols
steps.content: When coordination is impossible, the intersection controllers trigger **backup protocols**. These are pre-defined instructions that prioritize safety over efficiency. For example, the system might switch to a default safe operating state, such as all-way flashing red lights (acting like a 4-way stop) or flashing yellow lights to warn drivers to proceed with caution.

highlights.text: Implementation of default safe operating modes (e.g., all-way flashing yellow/red) ensures safety when coordination is impossible.

steps.title: Establish local autonomy
steps.content: Because the central controller can't send updates, each intersection relies on **local autonomy**. The hardware at the intersection uses pre-programmed timing patterns stored in its own memory. This ensures that even without a network connection, the lights continue to function in a predictable way for motorists, fulfilling the requirement for basic safety.

highlights.text: Intersection nodes operate independently using pre-programmed timing patterns when communication with the central controller is lost.

steps.title: Final summary of marks
steps.content: To earn the full 2 marks, you should explain that the system switches to **local autonomy** using pre-programmed patterns and implements **backup protocols** (like flashing lights) to maintain safety when coordination fails. This is a classic example of **Control Systems in Real-World Applications** (Topic A1.3.7).

questions.specification: A hospital uses a real-time control system for patient monitoring.

parts.content: Define what is meant by a "real-time system".
parts.markscheme:
- Define a real-time system as a system that must respond to events within specific and guaranteed time constraints <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that the correctness of the system depends not only on the logical result but also on the time of delivery <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe how the system must meet deadlines for critical operations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that the response time is predictable and bounded <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award a maximum of 2 marks.

parts.content: Distinguish between hard real-time and soft real-time systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Distinguish hard real-time systems where missing a deadline results in system failure or catastrophic consequences <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Distinguish soft real-time systems where missing occasional deadlines is tolerable or causes degraded performance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provide examples: hard systems include medical life support or nuclear reactor control; soft systems include video streaming or audio playback <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify requirements: hard systems require guaranteed response times, while soft systems require best-effort timing <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award a maximum of 3 marks.

parts.content: State three characteristics that make real-time systems suitable for medical applications.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify reliability as a characteristic, where the system must operate without failure for patient safety <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State the importance of predictable response times for patient monitoring and alerts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain high availability as the requirement for the system to be operational 24/7 <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe fault tolerance as the ability to continue operating despite component failures <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify data integrity as ensuring accurate data processing and storage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mention security as the protection of patient data and system access <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award a maximum of 3 marks.

questions.specification:
A manufacturing control system coordinates multiple robotic assembly lines.

**Workflow details (for this question):**
- **Line D (Material Supply)** provides raw materials from the **raw material stock** to **Line A**.
- **Line A (Part Assembly)** uses the **conveyor belt** to assemble parts. Line A can only start once materials from Line D are available.
- When Line A completes, **Line B (Quality Check)** tests the assembled parts at the **testing station**.
- When Line B completes, **Line C (Packaging)** packages the checked parts and requires **packaging materials**.

Assume the control system must:
- prevent two lines using the same shared resource at the same time (e.g. conveyor belt), and
- enforce the stated operation order (D supplies → A assembles → B checks → C packages).

parts.content:
Design a process synchronization table for coordinating assembly line operations:

| Assembly Line | Operation Type | Duration | Dependencies | Shared Resources | Synchronization Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Line A | Part Assembly | 45 sec | | Conveyor Belt | |
| Line B | Quality Check | 30 sec | Line A Complete | Testing Station | |
| Line C | Packaging | 20 sec | Line B Complete | | |
| Line D | Material Supply | 60 sec | | Raw Materials | |
parts.markscheme:
- Line A dependency stated as "Line D materials" (or equivalent) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Line A synchronization method given as "Mutex lock" for exclusive access to conveyor belt <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Line B synchronization method given as "Semaphore" <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Line C shared resource filled as "Packaging materials" <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Line C synchronization method given as "Semaphore" <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Line D dependency stated as "Raw material stock" (or equivalent) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyze how operating-system techniques would be used to prevent resource conflicts and ensure proper sequencing of operations.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that **mutual exclusion** is needed to ensure only one process/line uses a shared resource at a time (e.g. conveyor belt) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe the use of a **counting mechanism** to represent the number of available identical resources (e.g. testing stations or packaging materials) and block/wake processes accordingly <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify a **deadlock prevention/avoidance** approach such as consistent resource ordering to prevent circular wait <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Analyze the **producer–consumer style sequencing** where output from one line becomes input to the next, requiring signalling when an operation completes before the next begins <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Discuss management of **finite resource pools** (tracking allocation and release so resources are returned for later use) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each valid analysis point up to 4 marks maximum.</note>

questions.specification: Process scheduling is essential for efficient system operation.

parts.content: Define the term "process scheduling" in operating systems.
parts.markscheme:
- State that it is the method used by the operating system to determine which process gets CPU time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that it is an algorithm that decides the order and duration of process execution <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that it is the management of process execution to optimize system performance <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award a maximum of 1 mark for any valid definition.</note>

parts.content: Compare First Come First Served (FCFS) and Shortest Job First (SJF) scheduling algorithms.
parts.markscheme:
- Processes are executed in the order of their arrival (FCFS) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Processes with the shortest burst time are executed first (SJF) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- FCFS is simple to implement but can cause long waiting times (the convoy effect) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- SJF minimizes the average waiting time for a set of processes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- SJF may cause starvation of longer processes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- FCFS is inherently non-preemptive <marks>1 mark</marks>
- SJF can be either preemptive or non-preemptive <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each correct comparison up to a maximum of 4 marks.</note>

parts.content: Explain why priority-based scheduling might lead to starvation.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that low-priority processes may never receive CPU time if higher-priority tasks continuously arrive <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify that high-priority processes can continuously preempt lower-priority ones <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that the system becomes unfair as certain processes are indefinitely delayed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify that aging is the solution, where the priority of a waiting process is gradually increased over time <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each correct point up to a maximum of 3 marks.</note>

questions.specification: File management is a crucial function of operating systems.

parts.content: Outline the purpose of a file allocation table (FAT).
parts.markscheme:
- Tracks which disk sectors or clusters are allocated to specific files <marks>1</marks>
- Maps the physical locations of files on the storage device <marks>1</marks>
- Keeps a record of free and used disk space <marks>1</marks>
- Maintains links between clusters to manage fragmented files <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the FAT's role
steps.content: In the context of **Topic A1.3.2: Functions of an Operating System**, file management is a primary task. A File Allocation Table (FAT) is essentially a database or a "map" that the operating system uses to keep track of where files are stored on a physical disk.

steps.title: Mapping physical locations
steps.content: The primary purpose of the FAT is to **map** the logical file names used by the user to their actual **physical locations** (clusters or sectors) on the storage device. Without this map, the operating system would not know which physical parts of the disk belong to which file.

highlights.text: Maps the physical locations of files on the storage device

steps.title: Tracking storage status
steps.content: The FAT also performs **status tracking**. It maintains a constant record of every sector on the disk to identify which are **allocated** (used by a file) and which are **free** (available for new data). This ensures that when you save a new file, the OS can quickly find empty space and avoid overwriting existing data.

highlights.text: Keeps a record of free and used disk space

steps.title: Managing fragmented files
steps.content: Finally, because files are often split into different parts across the disk (fragmentation), the FAT maintains **links** between these parts. It tracks the sequence of clusters so the operating system can read a file from start to finish even if it isn't stored in one continuous block.

highlights.text: Maintains links between clusters to manage fragmented files

steps.title: Final Mark Summary
steps.content:
To earn the **2 marks** for this question, you should outline two distinct points regarding its purpose:

1.  **Mapping:** Identifying the physical sectors where files reside.
2.  **Tracking:** Keeping a record of which space is free versus which is occupied.

steps.title: Identify the OS function
steps.content: In **Topic A1.3: Operating systems and control systems**, we learn that one of the primary functions of an Operating System is **file management**. A File Allocation Table (FAT) is a specific method used by the OS to keep track of how data is stored on a hard drive or flash drive.

highlights.text: File management is a crucial function of operating systems.

steps.title: Mapping clusters and sectors
steps.content: Storage devices are divided into small, fixed-size blocks called **clusters**. The primary purpose of the FAT is to act as a map or index that tracks which specific clusters are allocated to which files. Without this, the OS wouldn't know where a file actually 'lives' on the physical hardware.

highlights.text: Tracks which disk sectors or clusters are allocated to specific files

steps.title: Understanding cluster chaining
steps.content:
Files are often too large to fit into a single cluster. The FAT manages this by using **cluster chaining**. Each entry in the table for a specific cluster contains the address of the *next* cluster in the file. 

Think of it like a **linked list**: 
- Cluster $102$ points to Cluster $103$
- Cluster $103$ points to Cluster $250$
- Cluster $250$ contains an 'End of File' (EOF) marker.

This 'chain' allows the OS to find all parts of a file, even if they are scattered across the disk.

highlights.text: Maintains links between clusters to manage fragmented files

steps.title: Managing file fragmentation
steps.content:
Over time, as files are deleted and new ones are saved, a disk becomes **fragmented**. This means a single file might be stored in non-contiguous (separated) blocks. 

The FAT is essential here because it allows the OS to reconstruct these fragmented files by following the cluster chain. It doesn't matter if the pieces are far apart; the FAT provides the pointers needed to read the file sequentially.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks, you should outline that the FAT:
1.  **Identifies the physical location** of files by tracking which clusters are used.
2.  **Manages fragmentation** by maintaining a 'chain' (linked list) of cluster addresses so the OS can reconstruct files stored in non-contiguous blocks.

parts.content: Compare hierarchical and flat file systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Hierarchical systems use directories to organize files in a tree structure, whereas flat systems store all files in a single directory <marks>1</marks>
- Hierarchical systems offer better organization and navigation for many files, while flat systems are simpler but become difficult to manage as file counts grow <marks>1</marks>
- Hierarchical systems support the creation of subdirectories, which are not allowed in flat file systems <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the Organizational Structure
steps.content:
The primary difference lies in how files are logically arranged. 

*   **Hierarchical file systems** use a **tree structure**, where files are organized into a nested system of folders and subfolders. 
*   **Flat file systems** use a **single directory** (or root level) where every file is stored in the same place without any grouping.

highlights.text: Hierarchical systems use directories to organize files in a tree structure, whereas flat systems store all files in a single directory

steps.title: Compare Subdirectory Support
steps.content:
Technical flexibility regarding "folders within folders" differs significantly between the two:

*   **Hierarchical systems** allow for the creation of **subdirectories**, enabling a parent-child relationship between folders.
*   **Flat file systems** do **not allow subdirectories**; all files exist at the same depth, which limits the ability to categorize data.

highlights.text: Hierarchical systems support the creation of subdirectories, which are not allowed in flat file systems

steps.title: Evaluate Scalability and Management
steps.content:
When comparing how these systems handle growth:

*   **Hierarchical systems** are highly **scalable** and offer efficient navigation for large volumes of data because related files can be grouped.
*   **Flat systems** are simpler to implement but become **difficult to manage** as the number of files increases, as users must search through one long list of files.

highlights.text: Hierarchical systems offer better organization and navigation for many files, while flat systems are simpler but become difficult to manage as file counts grow

steps.title: Define the organizational structures
steps.content:
To compare these two, we first look at how they physically arrange data. A **hierarchical file system** uses a tree-like structure where files are organized into directories and subdirectories. In contrast, a **flat file system** stores all files in a single, central directory with no subfolders allowed.

This fundamental difference in structure is the root of how users interact with their data.

highlights.text: Hierarchical systems use directories to organize files in a tree structure, whereas flat systems store all files in a single directory

steps.title: Evaluate usability and navigation
steps.content:
From a **usability** perspective, hierarchical systems are much more intuitive for humans. They allow you to group related files (like putting all your 'Math' assignments in one folder), which makes navigation faster because you only look at a small subset of files at a time. 

In a flat system, you have to scroll through every single file to find what you need, which is only user-friendly if you have a very small number of files.

highlights.text: Hierarchical systems offer better organization and navigation for many files

steps.title: Assess scalability for growth
steps.content:
When we talk about **scalability**, we are looking at what happens as the number of files increases. 

*   **Flat systems** fail to scale well; as you add hundreds of files, managing them becomes a nightmare due to naming conflicts and "clutter."
*   **Hierarchical systems** scale beautifully because you can simply create new subdirectories to keep the system organized, no matter how many thousands of files you have.

highlights.text: flat systems are simpler but become difficult to manage as file counts grow

steps.title: Summary for the exam
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks, ensure you clearly state the structural difference and the resulting management impact:

1.  **Structure**: Hierarchical uses a tree/subdirectory structure, while flat uses a single level.
2.  **Management**: Hierarchical is better for organizing large volumes of data (high scalability), whereas flat becomes difficult to navigate as file counts grow.

steps.title: The Simple Flat System
steps.content: In early or simple operating systems, file management began with a **flat file system**. In this approach, every single file is stored in one single directory or container. While this is straightforward for a computer with very few files, it lacks any internal structure or categorization.

highlights.text: flat systems store all files in a single directory

steps.title: Limitations of Flat Files
steps.content:
As storage capacity grew, flat systems encountered significant limitations:
1. **Scalability:** Finding a specific file among thousands in a single list is incredibly slow for both the user and the OS.
2. **Naming Conflicts:** Because everything is in one place, every file must have a unique name. You couldn't have two different files named `data.txt` even if they belonged to different projects.

highlights.text: flat systems are simpler but become difficult to manage as file counts grow

steps.title: Evolution to Hierarchical Systems
steps.content: To solve these issues, operating systems evolved to use **hierarchical file systems**. This method organizes files into a **tree structure**. It introduces the concept of **subdirectories** (folders within folders), allowing users to group related files together logically. This evolution allows for better navigation and the ability to use the same filename in different directories.

highlights.text: Hierarchical systems use directories to organize files in a tree structure
Hierarchical systems support the creation of subdirectories

steps.title: Final Comparison for Marks
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks, your comparison should highlight these evolutionary differences:

*   **Structure:** Flat systems use a single directory, whereas hierarchical systems use a tree structure with subdirectories.
*   **Management:** Flat systems are simple but fail as file counts grow; hierarchical systems provide superior organization and navigation for large volumes of data.

parts.content: Explain why defragmentation may be necessary for hard disk drives.
parts.markscheme:
- Files become fragmented over time, being split across non-contiguous sectors <marks>1</marks>
- Fragmentation increases read/write head movement, which slows down file access times <marks>1</marks>
- Defragmentation reorganizes data into contiguous sectors to improve disk performance <marks>1</marks>
- The process reduces physical wear on the mechanical components of the drive <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the problem of fragmentation
steps.content: In a Hard Disk Drive (HDD), the operating system tries to save files in the first available space. Over time, as files are deleted and created, large files end up being split into pieces and stored in **non-contiguous sectors** (gaps that are not next to each other) across the disk platter. This state is known as fragmentation.

highlights.text: Files become fragmented over time, being split across non-contiguous sectors

steps.title: Analyze the mechanical impact
steps.content: Because HDDs are mechanical devices, they use a physical **read/write head** that moves across a spinning platter. When a file is fragmented, the head must move back and forth to different physical locations to read a single file. This increased mechanical movement creates significant delays (seek time and latency).

highlights.text: Fragmentation increases read/write head movement

steps.title: Connect to performance speed
steps.content: The result of this extra head movement is a noticeable **slowdown in file access times**. Defragmentation is necessary because it reorganizes these scattered pieces into a continuous, or **contiguous**, sequence. This allows the read/write head to read the file in one smooth motion, which dramatically improves the disk's performance and speed.

highlights.text: slows down file access times
Defragmentation reorganizes data into contiguous sectors to improve disk performance

steps.title: Define Fragmentation
steps.content: Over time, as files are created, deleted, and edited on a Hard Disk Drive (HDD), they often become **fragmented**. This means that a single file is no longer stored in one continuous block but is instead split across **non-contiguous sectors** located in different physical areas of the disk platters.

highlights.text: Files become fragmented over time, being split across non-contiguous sectors

steps.title: Analyze Mechanical Movement
steps.content:
Unlike Solid State Drives (SSDs), an HDD is a mechanical device with a physical **read/write head** that moves across spinning disks. 

When a file is fragmented, the head must physically move (or "seek") back and forth multiple times to find all the separate pieces of that file. This constant, rapid movement is the primary cause of mechanical strain during disk operations.

highlights.text: Fragmentation increases read/write head movement, which slows down file access times

steps.title: Explain Longevity Benefits
steps.content:
Defragmentation reorganizes the data so that file parts are placed in **contiguous sectors** (side-by-side). 

From a mechanical perspective, this is vital because it significantly **reduces the physical wear and tear** on the drive's moving components. By minimizing the amount of distance the read/write head has to travel, you extend the operational lifespan (longevity) of the drive.

highlights.text: Defragmentation reorganizes data into contiguous sectors to improve disk performance
The process reduces physical wear on the mechanical components of the drive

questions.specification: A multi-user database system requires careful resource management.

parts.content: Explain the concept of deadlock in operating systems and how it can occur.
parts.markscheme:
- Define deadlock as a situation where processes are blocked indefinitely while waiting for resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain circular wait where Process $A$ waits for a resource held by Process $B$, which in turn waits for a resource held by Process $A$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify mutual exclusion as a condition where resources cannot be shared simultaneously <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outline a database-related example such as transactions waiting for locks on different tables <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe three techniques that operating systems use to prevent or resolve deadlocks.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe prevention as a technique that requires processes to request all necessary resources at once <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe avoidance as a technique that only grants resource requests when it is safe to do so (i.e. it will not lead to deadlock) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe detection and recovery by periodically checking for deadlocks and terminating or restarting specific processes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe resource ordering where processes must request resources in a predetermined sequence <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe the use of timeouts to set time limits on resource requests <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe preemption as allowing the operating system to forcibly remove resources from a process <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how a mutex lock can be used to control access to shared resources.
parts.markscheme:
- Define a mutex lock as a mechanism to ensure mutual exclusion for a shared resource/critical section <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outline that a process must acquire the lock before entering the critical section (and waits/blocks if it is already locked) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outline that the process releases the lock after leaving the critical section so other processes can proceed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that this ensures only one process accesses the shared resource at a time, preventing race conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Give a relevant example (e.g. controlling access to a shared file, printer, or database table lock) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Modern operating systems manage multiple processes simultaneously.

parts.content: Define the term "process" in the context of operating systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Define a process as a program in execution or an instance of a program loaded in memory and being executed <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the core concept
steps.content: To answer this question, we need to look at **Topic A1.3: Operating systems**. In computer science, we distinguish between a **program**, which is a static set of instructions stored on a disk, and a **process**, which is what happens when those instructions are actually being carried out by the computer.

steps.title: Provide the technical definition
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Definition** approach, a process is technically defined as a **program in execution**. 

This means that the operating system has taken the static code from secondary storage and turned it into an active task that the CPU is currently processing.

highlights.text: program in execution

steps.title: Define by memory state
steps.content:
Alternatively, you can define a process as an **instance of a program loaded in memory**. 

When a program starts, the OS allocates resources (like RAM and CPU time) to it. Once it is loaded into the primary memory and is being handled by the scheduler, it is no longer just a file; it is a process.

highlights.text: instance of a program loaded in memory and being executed

parts.content: Describe three functions performed by an operating system.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe process management as the management of process creation, scheduling, and termination <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe memory management as the allocation and deallocation of memory spaces to processes <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe file management as the creation, deletion, and organization of files on secondary storage <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Accept other valid functions such as device management (controlling hardware devices), providing a user interface (GUI or CLI), security (protecting data), or network management.</note>

steps.title: Define Process Management
steps.content:
The operating system manages the lifecycle of all running programs. **Process management** involves the creation of processes when a program starts, scheduling them to ensure the CPU is used efficiently, and terminating them when they are no longer needed.

This aligns with **Topic A1.3.2: Functions of an Operating System**, specifically focusing on how the OS handles multiple active tasks simultaneously.

highlights.text: Describe three functions performed by an operating system.

steps.title: Define Memory Management
steps.content:
**Memory management** is the process of allocating and deallocating blocks of primary memory (RAM) to various processes. The OS ensures that each process has enough memory to run without interfering with other processes, and it reclaims that memory once the process terminates.

As covered in **Topic A1.1.4**, primary memory is a critical resource that the OS must optimize to keep the system running smoothly.

steps.title: Define File Management
steps.content:
**File management** involves the organization and tracking of data stored on secondary storage devices (like HDDs or SSDs). The OS handles the creation, deletion, and naming of files, as well as maintaining a directory structure (folders) so that data can be easily retrieved.

This function bridges the gap between the physical hardware described in **Topic A1.1.7** and the user's need to store information permanently.

steps.title: Review Markscheme Requirements
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure your descriptions include the core internal actions for each resource:

*   **Process Management:** Mention creation, scheduling, or termination.
*   **Memory Management:** Mention allocation or deallocation of RAM.
*   **File Management:** Mention creation, deletion, or organization on secondary storage.

steps.title: Identify OS roles
steps.content: To answer this using the **Interaction and Security Approach**, we focus on how the Operating System (OS) acts as a bridge between the user, the hardware, and the data. According to the IB syllabus (**Topic A1.3.2**), the OS performs several vital functions to manage these relationships.

highlights.text: Describe three functions performed by an operating system.

steps.title: Peripheral Device Management
steps.content: The OS manages **peripheral device interaction** by using device drivers. It coordinates how external hardware (like printers, keyboards, or monitors) communicates with the internal system. This ensures that when you plug in a device, the system recognizes it and manages the data flow between the CPU and the hardware.

highlights.text: device management (controlling hardware devices)

steps.title: Providing a User Interface
steps.content: The OS provides a **User Interface (UI)**, such as a Graphical User Interface (GUI) with icons and menus or a Command Line Interface (CLI) using text. This is the primary way a user interacts with the computer's complex hardware and software without needing to understand machine code.

highlights.text: providing a user interface (GUI or CLI)

steps.title: Security and Access Control
steps.content: The OS ensures **security** by managing user accounts and access rights. This includes authenticating users (e.g., via passwords or biometrics) and setting permissions to ensure that only authorized users can view or modify specific files, protecting the system from internal and external threats.

highlights.text: security (protecting data)

parts.content: Explain the concept of virtual memory and why it is useful.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that virtual memory uses a combination of physical RAM and secondary storage to simulate a larger memory space <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe the mechanism of paging or swapping where data is moved between RAM and disk as needed <marks>A1</marks>
- State that a benefit of virtual memory is allowing programs larger than the physical RAM capacity to run <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Accept other valid benefits such as enabling multitasking with limited physical memory, providing memory protection between processes, or achieving efficient memory utilization.</note>

steps.title: Defining Virtual Memory
steps.content:
Virtual memory is a memory management technique where the Operating System (OS) integrates **physical RAM** (primary memory) with **secondary storage** (such as an SSD or HDD). 

This creates a "logical" address space that appears much larger to applications than the actual physical RAM installed in the computer. This concept is covered in **Topic A1.3.2** regarding the functions of an Operating System.

highlights.text: Explain that virtual memory uses a combination of physical RAM and secondary storage to simulate a larger memory space

steps.title: Mechanism: Paging and Swapping
steps.content:
To manage this space, the OS uses a mechanism called **paging**. Memory is divided into small, fixed-size blocks called "pages."

- When the physical RAM becomes full, the OS identifies pages that are not currently being used by the CPU.
- These inactive pages are moved (or "swapped") from the RAM to a specific area on the secondary storage.
- When the program needs that data again, the OS swaps it back into a free frame in the RAM.

highlights.text: Describe the mechanism of paging or swapping where data is moved between RAM and disk as needed

steps.title: Benefit: Exceeding RAM Capacity
steps.content:
The primary benefit of virtual memory is that it allows the computer to run programs that are **larger than the physical RAM capacity**. 

Without virtual memory, a program requiring 16 GB of space could not run on a machine with only 8 GB of RAM. Virtual memory allows the OS to load only the necessary parts of the program into RAM at any given time, enabling multitasking and the execution of complex, data-heavy applications.

highlights.text: State that a benefit of virtual memory is allowing programs larger than the physical RAM capacity to run

parts.content: Compare pre-emptive and cooperative multitasking.
parts.markscheme:
- Contrast pre-emptive multitasking, where the OS can forcibly interrupt running processes, with cooperative multitasking, where processes must voluntarily yield control <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that pre-emptive multitasking prevents a single process from monopolizing the CPU, leading to a more responsive system than cooperative multitasking <marks>A1</marks>
- State that pre-emptive multitasking provides better system stability because it prevents a misbehaving program from freezing the entire system <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify Control Mechanisms
steps.content:
The fundamental difference lies in who holds the power to stop a running process. 

In **cooperative multitasking**, the process itself is in control; it must voluntarily "yield" or give back control to the Operating System (OS). In contrast, **pre-emptive multitasking** gives the OS the power to forcibly interrupt (pre-empt) a running process to allocate CPU time to another task, usually via a hardware timer or interrupt.

highlights.text: Contrast pre-emptive multitasking, where the OS can forcibly interrupt running processes, with cooperative multitasking, where processes must voluntarily yield control

steps.title: Analyze Resource Management
steps.content: When we look at how the CPU is shared, **pre-emptive multitasking** is significantly more efficient for modern users. Because the OS manages "time slices," it prevents a single "greedy" or intensive process from monopolizing the CPU. This leads to a much more **responsive system**, as multiple programs appear to run smoothly at the same time, unlike cooperative systems where a single busy process can cause other apps to lag or stutter.

highlights.text: Explain that pre-emptive multitasking prevents a single process from monopolizing the CPU, leading to a more responsive system than cooperative multitasking

steps.title: Evaluate System Stability
steps.content: System stability is a major factor in modern OS design. In a cooperative environment, if a program crashes or enters an infinite loop without yielding, the entire computer freezes because the OS cannot regain control. **Pre-emptive multitasking** solves this by allowing the OS to simply "cut off" the misbehaving program and move to the next task, ensuring that one bad application doesn't crash the whole system.

highlights.text: State that pre-emptive multitasking provides better system stability because it prevents a misbehaving program from freezing the entire system

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To secure all 3 marks, your comparison should clearly distinguish these three areas:

1.  **Control**: OS-driven (Pre-emptive) vs. Process-driven (Cooperative).
2.  **Responsiveness**: Preventing CPU monopoly ensures all tasks get time.
3.  **Stability**: The ability to recover from a frozen or crashed application.

This topic is covered in detail in **Topic A1.3: Operating systems and control systems**, specifically regarding how the OS manages multitasking and resource allocation.

questions.specification:
A cloud computing platform manages virtual machines (VMs) across three physical servers. The physical server specifications and VM requirements are provided below.

**Physical Servers:**
- **Server A:** 32 cores, 128 GB RAM, 4 TB storage
- **Server B:** 16 cores, 64 GB RAM, 2 TB storage
- **Server C:** 64 cores, 256 GB RAM, 8 TB storage

**VM Requirements:**
- **VM1:** 4 cores, 16 GB RAM, 500 GB storage
- **VM2:** 8 cores, 32 GB RAM, 1 TB storage
- **VM3:** 2 cores, 8 GB RAM, 250 GB storage
- **VM4:** 16 cores, 64 GB RAM, 2 TB storage

parts.content:
The VMs have been allocated to the physical servers as shown in the table below. Complete the table by calculating the utilization percentage for **CPU cores** and **RAM** for each server.

| Physical Server | CPU Cores | RAM (GB) | Storage (TB) | Current VMs | Utilization % (CPU, RAM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Server A | 32 | 128 | 4 | VM1, VM3 | |
| Server B | 16 | 64 | 2 | VM2 | |
| Server C | 64 | 256 | 8 | VM4 | |
parts.markscheme:
- Server A CPU utilization correctly calculated as $\frac{4+2}{32}=18.75\%$ (accept $18.8\%$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Server A RAM utilization correctly calculated as $\frac{16+8}{128}=18.75\%$ (accept $18.8\%$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Server B CPU utilization correctly calculated as $\frac{8}{16}=50\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Server B RAM utilization correctly calculated as $\frac{32}{64}=50\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Server C CPU utilization correctly calculated as $\frac{16}{64}=25\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Server C RAM utilization correctly calculated as $\frac{64}{256}=25\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how a hypervisor manages memory and ensures resource isolation between virtual machines.
parts.markscheme:
- Describes that each VM is given its own *virtual* memory/address space, so programs see memory as if it is dedicated to that VM <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that the hypervisor translates/maps a VM’s virtual addresses to physical RAM on the host (i.e., controls where VM memory is placed) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains isolation/protection: one VM cannot read or overwrite another VM’s memory because the hypervisor enforces access boundaries/permissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mentions managing limited RAM between VMs (e.g., setting limits/quotas, sharing unused memory, paging/swapping to disk when needed) to keep VMs stable and separate <marks>1 mark</marks>

topic.title: A1.3 Operating systems and control systems
